**White people in the United Kingdom** are a multi-ethnic group consisting of European UK residents who identify as and are perceived to be '[white people](/wiki/White_people "White people")'. White people constitute the historical and current majority of the people living in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), with 83.0% of the population identifying as white in the [2021 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census").

The [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") designates white people into several subgroups, with small terminology variations between the administrative jurisdictions of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Law_of_Northern_Ireland "Law of Northern Ireland"). These are local: [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish"), [White Gypsy or Irish Traveller](/wiki/White_Gypsy_or_Irish_Traveller "White Gypsy or Irish Traveller"), and immigrant descended [Other White](/wiki/Other_White "Other White"), and in Scotland; [White Polish](/wiki/White_Polish "White Polish"). In [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") ethnic group data is collected differently, where only the term 'White' is used, and with National Identity ('British', 'Irish', 'Northern Irish', or combinations) collected separately.

[British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") governs modern British citizenship and nationality, and can influence who may be defined, whether informally, in media and academia, or [UK Government](/wiki/UK_Government "UK Government") statistics, as *white Britons* or *white British people*. In censuses, millions of white people in the United Kingdom, who hold British citizenship, do not identify with the [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") [ethnicity classification](/wiki/Classification_of_ethnicity_in_the_United_Kingdom "Classification of ethnicity in the United Kingdom").

Outside of the census, white people in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") have been the subject of academic research, and have featured in public discourse in international and [British media](/wiki/Media_of_the_United_Kingdom "Media of the United Kingdom"), in which they often are identified as a broad racial or [social class](/wiki/Social_class_in_the_United_Kingdom "Social class in the United Kingdom") within the country.

## Terminology and background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology and background")]

In the 2021 census for England and Wales, 81.7% of the population identified as White, but only 74.4% identified as "White British" (inclusive of English, Welsh, Scottish, and Northern Irish).[[6]](#cite_note-7) This leaves a gap of roughly 7.3% of the total population - about 4.3 million people who identify as White but choose a different ethnic classification.

### Census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Census")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Districts_of_England_by_Other_White_percent.svg/250px-Districts_of_England_by_Other_White_percent.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Districts_of_England_by_Other_White_percent.svg)

Other White Population, people who identify as white persons who are not of the English, Welsh, Scottish, Roma, Irish or Irish Traveller ethnic groupings

Within the [Census in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Census_in_the_United_Kingdom "Census in the United Kingdom"), the Office for National Statistics collects information on white people who are resident in the United Kingdom, regardless of citizenship status. As censuses have progressed each decade, further categories have been included to accommodate subgroupings of white people in the country. As of the 2011 census, these subgroups are [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish"), [White Gypsy or Irish Traveller](/wiki/White_Gypsy_or_Irish_Traveller "White Gypsy or Irish Traveller"), [White Polish](/wiki/White_Polish "White Polish") (in Scotland), and [Other White](/wiki/Other_White "Other White"). There are small variations between the phrasing or terminology of these categories across the administrative regions of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), Scotland and Northern Ireland.[[7]](#cite_note-UK-White:Totals-8)

### Use in academia and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Use in academia and government")]

The [UK Government](/wiki/UK_Government "UK Government") uses the category of white people to help define and understand demography in the country. Devolved administrations, such as the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") and [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") make use of the racial category for social and equality impacts.[[8]](#cite_note-scotgov2020-9)[[9]](#cite_note-welshgov2007-10) Ministerial departments such as the [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office") and [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Justice (United Kingdom)"),[[10]](#cite_note-StateNation2020-11)[[11]](#cite_note-moj2016-12) non-ministerial, such as the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office") and office for [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London"),[[12]](#cite_note-UN2019-13)[[13]](#cite_note-mol2018-14) [HM Inspectorate of Constabulary](/wiki/HM_Inspectorate_of_Constabulary "HM Inspectorate of Constabulary"),[[14]](#cite_note-dupuy2017-15) and public bodies such as the [Equality and Human Rights Commission](/wiki/Equality_and_Human_Rights_Commission "Equality and Human Rights Commission"),[[15]](#cite_note-osborne2016-16) and [UK Statistics Authority](/wiki/UK_Statistics_Authority "UK Statistics Authority"), have produced research and analysis on what they have defined as white persons within the country.[[16]](#cite_note-Sleepwalking-17)[[17]](#cite_note-bowyer2011-18)

Academic, research and statistical organisations, such as [British Election Study](/wiki/British_Election_Study "British Election Study"), [NatCen Social Research](/wiki/NatCen_Social_Research "NatCen Social Research") and [ICM Research](/wiki/ICM_Research "ICM Research"), [Savanta ComRes](/wiki/Savanta_ComRes "Savanta ComRes"), define and categorise whites living in the UK, in order to study and poll respondents of all backgrounds.[[18]](#cite_note-Peace2015-19)[[19]](#cite_note-natcen-20)[[20]](#cite_note-icm-21)[[21]](#cite_note-greene2020-22) Think tanks, including the [Policy Studies Institute](/wiki/Policy_Studies_Institute "Policy Studies Institute"), [Resolution Foundation](/wiki/Resolution_Foundation "Resolution Foundation"), [Smith Institute](/wiki/Smith_Institute "Smith Institute") and [Social Issues Research Centre](/wiki/Social_Issues_Research_Centre "Social Issues Research Centre"), utilise the grouping of white people in the United Kingdom,[[22]](#cite_note-lin2020-23)[[8]](#cite_note-scotgov2020-9)[[23]](#cite_note-smith2015-24)[[24]](#cite_note-sirc2008-25) along with [Stroke Association](/wiki/Stroke_Association "Stroke Association") and [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress").[[25]](#cite_note-strokeassoc2018-26)[[26]](#cite_note-tuc2017-27)

Sociologists, social scientists, and academics of race and ethnicity, such as [Peter J. Aspinall](/wiki/Peter_J._Aspinall "Peter J. Aspinall"), [Richard Dyer](/wiki/Richard_Dyer "Richard Dyer") and [Mary J. Hickman](/wiki/Mary_J._Hickman "Mary J. Hickman") identify whites in Britain as an intersecting social and racial category.[[27]](#cite_note-fortier1982-28)[[28]](#cite_note-hickman1995-29)[[29]](#cite_note-aspinall2018-30)

#### White Irish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: White Irish")]

[University of the West of Scotland](/wiki/University_of_the_West_of_Scotland "University of the West of Scotland")'s Chris Gilligan argues that; "The idea that White people in the United Kingdom constitute a race or ethnic group is based on racialised thinking. It works with the logic of the race relations framework, it does not challenge it."[[30]](#cite_note-31) In her 2015 research, [University of Southampton](/wiki/University_of_Southampton "University of Southampton") fellow Rosalind Willis studied the intersection of the [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish") category in England, where there have been examples of distinctions made against the [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") which are culturally rejected or ignored.[[31]](#cite_note-32) In this regard, professor [Mary J. Hickman](/wiki/Mary_J._Hickman "Mary J. Hickman") has written how a combination of [othering](/wiki/Other_(philosophy)#Critical_theory "Other (philosophy)") the [ethnic Irish](/wiki/Ethnic_Irish "Ethnic Irish"), and a presupposition of the positivity of integration, has provided "tacit support for the 'myth of homogeneity' of white people in Britain".[[28]](#cite_note-hickman1995-29)

In July 2019, the [East Ham](/wiki/East_Ham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "East Ham (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") branch was criticised for its election of a white Irish woman as the women’s officer for its [Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic](/wiki/Black,_Asian_and_Minority_Ethnic "Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic") (BAME) forum. The woman in question self-identified as being an ethnic minority and no objections within the branch were raised against her election. Branch secretary, Syed Taqi Shah commented that "if somebody self-declares [as BAME], and the Labour Party allows them to do so, they should be respected."[[32]](#cite_note-HP1-33)[[33]](#cite_note-NR1-34)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/White_population_pyramid_2021.svg/250px-White_population_pyramid_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:White_population_pyramid_2021.svg)

White population in total as a population pyramid in 2021 (in England and Wales)

White people are the current and historical majority of the United Kingdom's population. The [2011 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census") recorded 55,010,359 of [White](/wiki/White "White") and 63,193 of [Gypsy/Traveller/Irish Traveller](/wiki/White_Gypsy_or_Irish_Traveller "White Gypsy or Irish Traveller") ethnicity, making a total white population of 55,073,552 or 87.2 per cent of the total population. These figures did not include self-reported people of mixed ethnicity.[[7]](#cite_note-UK-White:Totals-8)[[34]](#cite_note-Oakland-35)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

In the 1991 census, white people were recorded as the most likely group to have tertiary education. By the 2001 census, this had changed, with [British African-Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people") females, and [British Indian](/wiki/British_Indian "British Indian") men and women, becoming more likely to be qualified to that level.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Based on the 1994 [Policy Studies Institute](/wiki/Policy_Studies_Institute "Policy Studies Institute")'s NSEM survey, an [International Migration Review](/wiki/International_Migration_Review "International Migration Review")-published study determined a factor of this shift, finding that between the ages of 21–64, 13.8% of [British Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hinduism in the United Kingdom") held a higher education, versus what the study defined as 11.3% of "White Christians" in Britain.[[22]](#cite_note-lin2020-23) The [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government")'s 2007 *Minority Ethnic Youth Forum Report* found that, based on 2005 [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") data; "Interestingly, [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese"), [Black African](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people"), [Indian](/wiki/British_Indians "British Indians") and [Other Asian](/wiki/Asian_Britons "Asian Britons") groups are more likely to have degrees than white people in the UK."[[9]](#cite_note-welshgov2007-10) In England, since 2007, out of all ethnicities aged 18 years old who have received a state education, white pupils have had the lowest rate of entry into higher education and have also seen the lowest rate of increase, from 21.8% in 2007 to 32.2% in 2022. For comparison, 70.7% of [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") pupils and 50.6% of [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") pupils gained a higher education place in the same year.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: History")]

### British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: British Empire")]

The history of the racial classification of white people has its roots in the establishment of [European colonies](/wiki/History_of_colonialism "History of colonialism") in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), where they encountered and lived alongside [people of colour](/wiki/Person_of_color "Person of color"). The United Kingdom [[51]](#cite_note-52) Historian [Marika Sherwood](/wiki/Marika_Sherwood "Marika Sherwood") writes that while there is no implication that "all whites in Britain were or became imbued with racism"; the classification of the "white race" rose in the nineteenth century due in part to the increasing rise of the [eugenics](/wiki/Eugenics "Eugenics") and [scientific racism](/wiki/Scientific_racism "Scientific racism") movements of thought, with [anthropologists](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") classifying whites as distinct and separate race from other races such as Africans and Asians.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

After physician [John Fothergill](/wiki/John_Fothergill_(physician) "John Fothergill (physician)") disparagingly referred to them as "[nabobs](/wiki/Nabob "Nabob")"; in 1767, the *[Daily Gazetteer](/wiki/Daily_Gazetteer "Daily Gazetteer")*, within the context of their return to England, made the accusation that West Indian planters (such as those involved with the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company")) were corrupting Britain's political system, and who "being bred the tyrants of their slavish blacks, may endeavour to reduce the white [in Britain] to the same condition by an aristocracy".[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Interwar and post-WWII periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Interwar and post-WWII periods")]

Social scientist [Peter J. Aspinall](/wiki/Peter_J._Aspinall "Peter J. Aspinall") has analyzed how [interracial marriage in the UK](/wiki/Interracial_marriage#United_Kingdom "Interracial marriage"), as a phenomenon, caused societal reactions from whites in the interwar, and post-World War II periods. These included violence and racism against [African-American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") GIs, [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") seamen, and towards children from their relationships with white women. Aspinall, an expert in [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity"), wrote:[[29]](#cite_note-aspinall2018-30)

> Such experiences were shared by interracial people, couples and families throughout the twentieth century with their mere presence provoking or exacerbating the violence of white people in Britain, as evidenced during the numerous 'race riots', disturbances and attacks that occurred throughout the period.

This post-war period of history was recognised by [Mill Hill School](/wiki/Mill_Hill_School "Mill Hill School")'s marking of [Black History Month](/wiki/Black_History_Month#United_Kingdom_(1987) "Black History Month"), when the London school published a short history of the [Windrush generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation"), including the abuse received by arrivals such as [Floella Benjamin](/wiki/Floella_Benjamin "Floella Benjamin"), exploring how; "Unfortunately, many white people in Britain did not welcome the new arrivals and Floella, and many like her were faced with hatred and cruelty."[[54]](#cite_note-55) Politician [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell"), who became known for his anti-immigrant [Rivers of Blood speech](/wiki/Rivers_of_Blood_speech "Rivers of Blood speech"), has been identified by some media as an early source of defining white people as a racial interest group within Britain. In 1971, Powell had argued that "whites are being held back to accommodate the Asiatics and blacks".[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Late 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Late 20th century")]

According to the [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics")'s quarterly *Labour Force Survey*, in 1993-1994 white people undertook more employer‐funded training per capita than minority groups in the UK. Analysis by economists, Michael Shields and Stephen Wheatley Price, suggested that the situation may represent a failure in [United Kingdom employment equality laws](/wiki/United_Kingdom_employment_equality_law "United Kingdom employment equality law").[[56]](#cite_note-57) [Anthony Lester](/wiki/Anthony_Lester "Anthony Lester"), a key contributor to such legislation, stated in 1991 that "White people in Britain don't have the [legacy of guilt](/wiki/White_guilt "White guilt") about the past as there is in America about the period of [slavery](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade"), even though there is plenty to be guilty about".[[57]](#cite_note-58)

In another comparison of the two nations, while accepting that "any portrait of Britain as a haven of multicultural understanding and friendship among different groups is an exaggeration"; [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland "University of Maryland") professor Eric Uslaner has observed that "the effect of [segregation on civic norms](/wiki/Racial_segregation#United_Kingdom "Racial segregation") is far more pronounced for whites in the United States compared to whites in Britain."[[58]](#cite_note-59) Scholar [Ron Walters](/wiki/Ron_Walters "Ron Walters") also stressed the significance of correlation between white majorities and their behavioural patterns in the UK and US. Lecturer [Clarence Lusane](/wiki/Clarence_Lusane "Clarence Lusane") has written of Walter's use of themes, such as the "cultural similarity with regard to racial attitudes of Whites in Britain and the United States" and also, "the reception by the White host societies" towards black communities.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The 2008 [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") series *[White](/wiki/White_(TV_series) "White (TV series)")* attempted to address issues of white-related class and race issues in the country. Academic [Vron Ware](/wiki/Vron_Ware "Vron Ware") described the documentary as "a provocative series that claimed to address the marginality of working class white people in Britain."[[60]](#cite_note-61) Based on data from eligible voters, white people overall voted to leave the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2016's [Brexit referendum](/wiki/Brexit_referendum "Brexit referendum") in higher proportions than other racial groups in the country.[[10]](#cite_note-StateNation2020-11)

## Culture and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Culture and society")]

[University of Essex](/wiki/University_of_Essex "University of Essex") professor Richard Berthoud has proposed that between 1970 and 2000, white families in Britain progressed towards modern [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism"), and away from other traditional behaviours.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### Alcohol and smoking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Alcohol and smoking")]

Based on a 243-person study (103 whites, 83 [British Pakistanis](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis"), and 57 [British Indians](/wiki/British_Indians "British Indians")) in [Bradford, England](/wiki/Bradford,_England "Bradford, England") in 1988, white people had higher rates of drinking and smoking than [Asian British people](/wiki/Asian-British_people "Asian-British people").[[62]](#cite_note-63) This correlated with a study around 30 years later, which found that alcohol consumption is higher among whites in Britain than other groups. In 2017 the [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") office published that; "51 per cent of ethnic minorities, and 16 per cent of white people, in Britain in 2017 had not consumed alcohol in the last week."[[13]](#cite_note-mol2018-14) A 2018 [Stroke Association](/wiki/Stroke_Association "Stroke Association") report also found that white people had the highest levels of alcohol consumption and smoking in the UK.[[25]](#cite_note-strokeassoc2018-26) White people in England are more likely to drink at levels classed as 'hazardous, harmful or dependent' than all other ethnic groups.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Integration and representations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Integration and representations")]

White people are usually defined, in scholarly works and media, as the majority group in the country.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[17]](#cite_note-bowyer2011-18) It is not always clear whether majority-based terminology is dependent on cultural perceptions, statistics (such as [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") censuses and [UK Statistics Authority](/wiki/UK_Statistics_Authority "UK Statistics Authority")'s Citizenship Surveys) or a combination of multiple factors. The scope of the term of white people, or reference to a white majority within the UK, is sometimes a source of debate or controversy.[[65]](#cite_note-66) For example, [Richard Dyer](/wiki/Richard_Dyer "Richard Dyer")'s work, an academic who specialises in racial representations, suggests the ethnicity of white people in Britain is rarely scrutinised.[[27]](#cite_note-fortier1982-28)

There have been social challenges to integration, and adjustment to [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism"), in the United Kingdom among the white population. A study conducted by [NatCen Social Research](/wiki/NatCen_Social_Research "NatCen Social Research") asked whites in the UK a number of questions, including: "Do you think most white people in Britain would mind/would you mind if a close relative were to marry a person of black or West Indian/Asian origin?" The results found that between 1983 and 2013, the white participants' opinions on "white people in Britain" dropped from around 80% 'would mind' to under 60%, and their personal opinions moved from around 60% to just over 30%.[[19]](#cite_note-natcen-20)[[66]](#cite_note-67) A publicly-funded Citizenship Survey found that 56 per cent of "Whites in Britain" had friendships exclusively with other white people.[[16]](#cite_note-Sleepwalking-17) Based on analysis of the Citizenship Survey, academics Anthony Heath and Yaojun Li wrote:

> [I]t is in fact the whites who are by far the most likely to have friends only from their own race - that is other whites. Given the much larger number of whites in Britain, and the geographical concentration of ethnic minorities in large conurbations, many whites will not have opportunity to meet ethnic minorities.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

A 1982 study of riots in the UK in *[India Quarterly](/wiki/India_Quarterly "India Quarterly")*, outlined what it described as a "substantial displacement of the local whites in Britain." The journal suggested that "this has occurred in certain areas like [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Brixton](/wiki/Brixton "Brixton"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [Southall](/wiki/Southall "Southall"), [Toxteth](/wiki/Toxteth "Toxteth"), [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton")", and that "to a great extent this clustering has hampered the assimilation process."[[68]](#cite_note-69) After the [1991 United Kingdom census](/wiki/1991_United_Kingdom_census "1991 United Kingdom census"), newspaper *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* reported: "Yet Britain as a whole remains very white indeed; there is nothing "multicultural" about it. At the census in 1991, ethnic minorities came to about 5.5 per cent of the population: that is, just over three million in a total population of almost 55 million."[[69]](#cite_note-70)

In 2000, *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")* reported the demographic prediction that white people would become a minority group in all or certain parts of the UK, while remaining the largest singular group (which is sometimes defined as a [majority minority](/wiki/Majority_minority "Majority minority") scenario).
Governmental advisor, [Lee Jasper](/wiki/Lee_Jasper "Lee Jasper"), stated that "the demographics show that white people in [London](/wiki/London "London") will become a minority by 2010", and that 'We could have a majority black Britain by the turn of the century."[[70]](#cite_note-Observer2000-71) [Runnymede Trust](/wiki/Runnymede_Trust "Runnymede Trust"), a leading British race equality think tank, published criticism which took issue with assumptions of future birth rates, and an "inadequacy" in the "use of the term 'whites'."
[[71]](#cite_note-72)

A 2010 *[Ethnic and Racial Studies](/wiki/Ethnic_and_Racial_Studies "Ethnic and Racial Studies")* study, which analyzed UK and US census data, showed that Black people in Britain were more likely to have a UK born White partner or spouse than their US counterparts.[[72]](#cite_note-73) With regard to censuses, the [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") category has, at times, been the focus of demography (above other groupings, such as [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish")) particularly in journalistic media.[[28]](#cite_note-hickman1995-29) In 2012, *[The Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* reported that the percentage reduction in white people recorded at the 2011 census had occurred "despite an influx" of [White Polish](/wiki/White_Polish "White Polish") people.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

## Social and political issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Social and political issues")]

### Employment and housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Employment and housing")]

A 2005 [Smith Institute](/wiki/Smith_Institute "Smith Institute") report on migration noted that income, and employment rates, of "British-born white individuals" and "foreign-born whites" were similar in the United Kingdom, and diverged almost inseparably in comparison with "non-white immigrants".[[23]](#cite_note-smith2015-24) According to the [Social Issues Research Centre](/wiki/Social_Issues_Research_Centre "Social Issues Research Centre"), there is still, however, significant diversity within the white populace in terms of income. In 2008, the [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford")-based institute also reported that whites experienced half the rates of low income households as do ethnic minorities.[[24]](#cite_note-sirc2008-25) In 2009, writing for a [Runnymede Trust](/wiki/Runnymede_Trust "Runnymede Trust") publication, [University of Iceland](/wiki/University_of_Iceland "University of Iceland") researcher Kjartan Sveinsson wrote:[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

> Feigning white working-class disadvantage as an ethnic disadvantage rather than as class disadvantage is exactly what rhetorically places this group in direct competition with minority ethnic groups. As such, it does little to address the real and legitimate grievances poor white people in Britain have.

Between 2012 and 2013, the [Equality and Human Rights Commission](/wiki/Equality_and_Human_Rights_Commission "Equality and Human Rights Commission") found that white people were far less likely to live in poverty, compared with what the report defined as "Ethnic minority people".[[15]](#cite_note-osborne2016-16) [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") research in 2017,[[26]](#cite_note-tuc2017-27) which was analyzed by the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), showed that white people had higher rates of general employment and less chance of insecure employment, such as seasonal or agency work, than other racial groups in the Britain.[[8]](#cite_note-scotgov2020-9) A 2019 study found that on income-related demography, [British Indians](/wiki/British_Indians "British Indians") are the only census-based ethnic group with similar earnings to white people in the UK.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

White people in the United Kingdom have the lowest rates of household crowding, with two per cent of the population experiencing it.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Discrimination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Discrimination")]

Further information: [Anti-white racism § United Kingdom](/wiki/Anti-white_racism#United_Kingdom "Anti-white racism")

White people face less discrimination in the United Kingdom than ethnic minorities. For example, [European Network Against Racism](/wiki/European_Network_Against_Racism "European Network Against Racism") notes that white people in the country are around six times less likely than black people, and half as likely as Asians, to be stopped by police.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The [British Election Study](/wiki/British_Election_Study "British Election Study") has defined "white people" in Britain, as part of a poll of 2,049 ethnic minority respondents regarding opinions on equal opportunity in British society.[[18]](#cite_note-Peace2015-19)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

The [Centre for Economic Performance](/wiki/Centre_for_Economic_Performance "Centre for Economic Performance") revealed in a 2014 study that "Many white people in the UK feel that social landlords actively discriminate against them in favour of immigrants and ethnic minorities." Analyzing this trend, professor [Alan Manning](/wiki/Alan_Manning "Alan Manning") found "no basis in reality for this perceived discrimination".[[80]](#cite_note-cep2014-81) [Mona Chalabi](/wiki/Mona_Chalabi "Mona Chalabi"), a notable [data journalist](/wiki/Data_journalism "Data journalism"), writing in 2015, suggested that while racism from white people in the UK has a greater significance than from ethnic minorities; "a lot of other white people in Britain genuinely believe racism affects them too".[[81]](#cite_note-82) Journalist [Simon Kelner](/wiki/Simon_Kelner "Simon Kelner") has stated a similar view, posing the question "Can white people in Britain really feel they're the victims of racism?"[[82]](#cite_note-83)

In 2016, the [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Justice (United Kingdom)") issued a report noting that "white people in Britain" were four times less likely to be in prison than [black Britons](/wiki/Black_Britons "Black Britons").[[11]](#cite_note-moj2016-12) In 2017, [HM Inspectorate of Constabulary](/wiki/HM_Inspectorate_of_Constabulary "HM Inspectorate of Constabulary") found by studying [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") data that; "White people in the United Kingdom are more likely to be carrying drugs when stopped by police but less likely to be stopped, compared to black people who are disproportionately searched".[[14]](#cite_note-dupuy2017-15) Between 2017 and 2018, [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office") data revealed that [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") was the county where police were least likely to question white people in comparison with ethnic minority groups. Whites had around 17 times less chance of being subjected to [Stop and Search](/wiki/Stop_and_Search "Stop and Search").[[10]](#cite_note-StateNation2020-11)

In a 2018 [ICM Research](/wiki/ICM_Research "ICM Research") poll conducted between 2013 and 2018, 4% of white people in the United Kingdom believed they had been treated like a shoplifter; 9% asked to leave an establishment for what seemed like no good reason; 18% believed they had been unfairly overlooked for a job; 52% felt a stranger was rude or abusive to them. (The results were 47% (treated like shoplifter), 25% (asked to leave), 43% (overlooked for job), and 69% (received abuse), respectively, for members of the [BAME](/wiki/Black,_Asian_and_minority_ethnic "Black, Asian and minority ethnic") community).[[20]](#cite_note-icm-21) A 2019 [United Nations Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council") report noted that the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office")'s Race Disparity Unit had number of findings in relation to racism, including that; "One of the primary discoveries made through the audit had been that ethnic minorities were worse off than white people in the United Kingdom."[[12]](#cite_note-UN2019-13) In 2020, a [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") and [Savanta ComRes](/wiki/Savanta_ComRes "Savanta ComRes") survey revealed that, among many other findings, that around half of white people in the country believed there was a fair representation of ethnic minorities in film and television (while 17 per cent of black British people agreed). Whites were also twice as likely to say they had been treated with respect by [British police](/wiki/British_police "British police").[[21]](#cite_note-greene2020-22)

In a 1995 study, sociologist [Jock Young](/wiki/Jock_Young "Jock Young") found that of 1000 randomly selected residents of Finsbury Park when asked if they had been stopped by the police over the past year, the [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish") population was disproportionately affected with 14.3%, in contrast to 12.8% of [Black Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people") and 5.8% of White British people. The researchers found the Police tactic of 'lurking and larking', whereby constables would wait outside Irish pubs and clubs to make arrests to be to blame for the high statistics, which was labelled a form of 'institutional racism'.[[83]](#cite_note-PTS1-84)[[84]](#cite_note-Runnymede1-85)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Health")]

1983 research of [breast cancer](/wiki/Breast_cancer "Breast cancer") rates in [Birmingham, England](/wiki/Birmingham,_England "Birmingham, England") found that whites in the United Kingdom had significantly higher rates of the disease than black and Asian people who had migrated to the country. The study examined white people who were born in either the UK or [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").[[85]](#cite_note-86) A 1999 study revealed that white people in the country had lower mortality rates from [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") than black people.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

In 2007, professor [Andrew Hattersley](/wiki/Andrew_Hattersley "Andrew Hattersley") studied the genomes of whites in the United Kingdom (as well as Finland and Italy), discovering what some researchers described as the first clear genetic link, via the [FTO gene](/wiki/FTO_gene "FTO gene"), to [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"). Presence of the gene beyond that initial subject pool was not yet studied.[[87]](#cite_note-cbc2007-88)[[88]](#cite_note-bbc2007-89)

White people are the most likely racial group to have a form of [atrial fibrillation](/wiki/Atrial_fibrillation "Atrial fibrillation") conditions. The same 2018 data also showed that whites were around half as likely to suffer a [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") than black people in Britain.[[25]](#cite_note-strokeassoc2018-26) Among a number of other disparities, 2019 research demonstrated that whites in Britain were prescribed [antipsychotic](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic") drugs (as [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") treatment) for around 4 weeks less on average than black Britons, placing the latter into an excess of dosage recommendations.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Research published in 2022, found that white people in England are more likely to develop cancer than black, mixed and Asian ethnic groups. Overall, compared to the white population, cancer rates were 40% lower in those of mixed-heritage, 38% lower in Asian people and 4% lower in black people.[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92) Generally, whites in England and Wales are more likely to die of cancer than their black or Asian counterparts.[[92]](#cite_note-:0-93)

Analysis published in 2023 comparing the risk of dying by suicide across sociodemographic groups in England and Wales reveal suicide rates were highest in the white (and mixed) ethnic groups for both men and women.[[93]](#cite_note-94) White people in England are also more likely to have self-harmed than people from the Asian, mixed and black ethnic groups.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

White people are less likely to die from [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") than any other racial group in the United Kingdom.[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97) White people in the country had half the chance of dying of the virus when compared with [black Britons](/wiki/Black_Britons "Black Britons"), while [British Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") showed similar fatality rates to white people. White males were at just under half the risk of men of [British Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi") and [British Pakistanis](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis") ancestry.[[97]](#cite_note-98) A British government report confirms that Black and Asian people in Britain had higher death rates amid the COVID-19 pandemic than their white counterparts.[[98]](#cite_note-Vakil-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) Despite the effects of COVID-19, whites in England and Wales have a higher overall [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") than any other ethnicity.[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) Whites in England and Wales have a lower [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth than nearly all other ethnic groups.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[92]](#cite_note-:0-93)

A 2009 study published in *[Ethnicity & Health](/wiki/Ethnicity_%26_Health "Ethnicity & Health")* demonstrated that the [White Irish](/wiki/White_Irish "White Irish") people self-reported higher rates of poor general health than the [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British") populace. This was found to be particularly the case in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), for those who had designated themselves as White, and with an "Irish" national identity.[[104]](#cite_note-105) In 2020, a [UCL](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") study based in [NHS England](/wiki/NHS_England "NHS England") data, showed that the White Irish group was around 50 per cent less at risk of death from COVID-19 than other [black, Asian and minority ethnic](/wiki/Black,_Asian_and_minority_ethnic "Black, Asian and minority ethnic") (BAME) groups. This was significantly lower than the White British group, which were 12 per cent lower than the average risk for BAME communities.[[105]](#cite_note-106)
